planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before transplanting (after soil warms to 70°F+) in well-drained, average to poor, neutral soil in full sun; tithonia is extremely heat- and drought-tolerant once established and actually blooms better in lean, dry soil.

directions: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before transplanting (after soil warms to 70°F+) in well-drained, average to poor, neutral soil in full sun; tithonia is extremely heat- and drought-tolerant once established and actually blooms better in lean, dry soil. Tithonia rotundifolia is the premier late-summer annual for attracting monarch butterflies, swallowtails, and native bumblebees — in high summer and early fall, a patch of blooming Mexican sunflowers will be covered with butterflies from dawn to dusk. The plant produces vivid, velvety, 3-inch, orange-scarlet flowers with a golden-yellow center on tall, branching, slightly hairy stems from midsummer through frost; hollow stems must be seared before using as cut flowers to prevent wilting. It is an outstanding back-of-border annual for hot, sunny gardens in zones 9–11 and is perennial in frost-free areas.

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost date, or direct sow outdoors 2 weeks after last frost, in well-drained, average soil in full sun; globe amaranth thrives in heat and humidity and is one of the most reliable summer annuals for zone 8–11 where summer heat defeats most other annuals.

directions: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost date, or direct sow outdoors 2 weeks after last frost, in well-drained, average soil in full sun; globe amaranth thrives in heat and humidity and is one of the most reliable summer annuals for zone 8–11 where summer heat defeats most other annuals. Gomphrena globosa is a tropical annual grown for its papery, clover-like, globe-shaped flower heads in vivid magenta-purple, pink, white, and orange that bloom continuously from midsummer through frost with virtually no care; the flower heads retain their color perfectly when dried, making it one of the best everlasting annuals — simply hang upside down to dry for wreaths, arrangements, and crafts. The flowers are excellent for small native bees and bumblebees; the plant is extraordinarily heat-tolerant and largely pest- and disease-free. An outstanding plant for hot-summer gardens and zone 9–11 landscapes.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost (or start indoors 3–4 weeks before) in well-drained, poor to average, neutral soil in full sun; sulphur cosmos is even more heat- and drought-tolerant than C. bipinnatus and blooms most freely in lean conditions — do not fertilize.

directions: Direct sow after last frost (or start indoors 3–4 weeks before) in well-drained, poor to average, neutral soil in full sun; sulphur cosmos is even more heat- and drought-tolerant than C. bipinnatus and blooms most freely in lean conditions — do not fertilize. Cosmos sulphureus is the warm-colored cosmos native to tropical Mexico and Central America, producing semi-double to double flowers in vivid yellow, gold, orange, and red on compact, branching plants; it is much more heat-tolerant than the familiar pink-and-white C. bipinnatus and provides brilliant color through the hottest summer periods when other cosmos decline. The flowers are excellent for small native bees, hoverflies, and monarch butterflies; sulphur cosmos is particularly heat-adapted and is the better cosmos choice for zone 8–11 southern gardens. It self-seeds prolifically in warm climates.

planting.note: Surface sow (seeds need light) indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost on moist seed-starting mix; do not cover seeds. Transplant to partial shade to full sun in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil after last frost.

directions: Surface sow (seeds need light) indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost on moist seed-starting mix; do not cover seeds. Transplant to partial shade to full sun in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil after last frost. Nicotiana sylvestris is a spectacular ornamental tobacco from northwestern Argentina, producing enormous (up to 24 inches wide), rough-hairy, basal rosettes of bold, dark-green leaves and tall, candelabra-like clusters of long, pendant, tubular white flowers in early summer that are intensely, sweetly fragrant — particularly at dusk and night, when the fragrance carries 50 feet or more. The flowers are adapted to sphinx moth pollination and their nocturnal fragrance attracts hawkmoths in the evening; they also attract hummingbirds. All parts are toxic; do not ingest. A spectacular annual for the back of shaded or partially shaded borders, where its bold texture and evening fragrance create a dramatic effect.

planting.note: Surface sow indoors (seeds need light) 6–8 weeks before last frost; do not cover seeds; transplant to well-drained, moderately fertile, neutral to slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun after last frost.

directions: Surface sow indoors (seeds need light) 6–8 weeks before last frost; do not cover seeds; transplant to well-drained, moderately fertile, neutral to slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun after last frost. Ageratum houstonianum is one of the few reliable blue-flowered annuals for summer borders, producing dense, rounded clusters of fuzzy, powder-blue to lilac-blue (and in cultivars, white and pink) flowers from early summer through frost; the blue forms are invaluable as a true-blue counterpoint to yellow and orange summer annuals and are excellent for creating blue-and-white formal bedding schemes. Tolerates partial shade better than most summer annuals, making it useful for bright, open shade situations; it is a good companion planting around cabbage and brassicas as a potential pest deterrent. The species can be mildly allelopathic and self-toxic in dense plantings.
